coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of the thermal and cycling behavior of a lithium titanate-based lithium-ion pouch cell
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hall, Florian
Touzri, Jonas
Wußler, Sabine
Buqa, Hilmi
Bessler, Wolfgang G.
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion pouch cells with lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) anode and lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2, NCA) cathode were investigated experimentally with respect to their electrical (0.1C…4C), thermal (5 °C…50 °C) and long-time cycling behavior. The 16 Ah cell exhibits an asymmetric charge/discharge behavior which leads to a strong capacity-rate effect, as well as a significantly temperature-dependent capacity (0.37 Ah ∙ K−1) which expresses as additional high-temperature feature in the differential voltage plot. The cell was cycled for 10,000 cycles inbetween the nominal voltage limits (1.7–2.7 V) with a symmetric 4C constant-current charge/discharge protocol, corresponding to approx. 3400 equivalent full cycles. A small (0.192 mΩ/1000 cycles) but continuous increase of internal resistance was observed. Using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), this could be identified to be caused by the NCA cathode, while the LTO anode showed only minor changes during cycling. The temperature-corrected capacity during 4C cycling exhibited a decrease of 1.28%/1000 cycles. The 1C discharge capacity faded by only 4.0% for CC discharge and 2.3% for CCCV discharge after 10,000 cycles. The cell thus exhibits very good internal-resistance stability and excellent capacity retention even under harsh (4C continuous) cycling, demonstrating the excellent stability of LTO as anode material.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: BMBF - German Federal Ministry of Education and Research
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Lithium titanate (LTO)
Lifetime
Ageing
Experiments

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the German Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) in the framework of the TempOLadung project (Grant no. 03FH013PX3 ) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Sebastian Ewinger and Christian Kupper (Offenburg University of Applied Sciences) for their support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Discharge/charge characteristics of the LTO/NCA pouch cell. a) Different C-rates at 20 °C ambient temperature; b) 4C at different ambient temperatures; c) 0.1C at different ambient temperatures. Note the cells were first charged then discharged, both with CCCV protocol; the charge throughput was set to zero for the fully-charged state as reference.

Cell surface temperature as function of time. a) Different C-rates at 20 °C ambient temperature; b) 4C at different ambient temperatures; c) 0.1C at different ambient temperatures (curves smoothed using a 300 s moving median filter and normalized to the temperature at the end of the rest phase following the charge phase). The data correspond to voltage-capacity curves shown in Fig. 1. Note the cells were first charged then discharged, both with CCCV protocol.

Temperature dependence of 4C constant-current discharge capacity. a) Capacity as function of cell surface temperature for different ambient temperatures. The solid line is shown as guide to the eye. b) Capacity resulting from a slow ambient temperature variation between 19 and 21 °C during continuous 4C cycling. These data correspond to the cycles indicated with an asterisk in Fig. 5a. The temperature values represent temporal averages during discharge.

Differential voltage analysis for 0.1C discharge. The data are based on Fig. 1c. The steep parts of the curves from Fig. 1c towards high and low charge throughput are not shown on the scale of this figure (dV/dC <−0.1 V/Ah).

Cycling with 4C constant-current discharge/charge (no rest). a) Measured discharge capacity, b) discharge-cycle averaged cell surface temperature, c) temperature-corrected discharge capacity (cf. text). The asterisk indicates the time of the experiments shown in Fig. 3b.

Cell characterization after each 1000 cycles: Discharge capacity at 20 °C and different C-rates, a) for the full CCCV discharge, b) for the CC part only. The solid lines are shown as guide to the eye.

Cell characterization after each 1000 cycles: a) Internal resistance and b), c) electrochemical impedance spectra at 20 °C and 50% SOC. The solid lines are shown as guide to the eye. Internal resistance and EIS measurements were not performed at 6000 cycles.

Results from the equivalent circuit fit to the experimental EIS shown in Fig. 7. a) experimental data and equivalent circuit fit results, b)–d) equivalent circuit parameters. The error bars are based on a maximum likelihood estimation, specifically the square roots of the diagonal terms in the variance-covariance matrix resulting from the Simplex fitting algorithm, as provided by the fitting software (cf. Section 2.2). The inset in panel d) shows the used equivalent circuit.
Electrochemical impedance spectra of a fresh cell at 20 °C for different SOC: a) Nyquist, b) Bode plots.

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation of the thermal and cycling behavior of a lithium titanate-based lithium-ion pouch cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Energy Systems Technology (INES), Offenburg University of Applied Sciences, Badstrasse 24, 77652 Offenburg, Germany

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present affiliation: Leclanché GmbH, Willstätt, Germany.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion pouch cells with lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) anode and lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2, NCA) cathode were investigated experimentally with respect to their electrical (0.1C…4C), thermal (5 °C…50 °C) and long-time cycling behavior. The 16 Ah cell exhibits an asymmetric charge/discharge behavior which leads to a strong capacity-rate effect, as well as a significantly temperature-dependent capacity (0.37 Ah ∙ K−1) which expresses as additional high-temperature feature in the differential voltage plot. The cell was cycled for 10,000 cycles inbetween the nominal voltage limits (1.7–2.7 V) with a symmetric 4C constant-current charge/discharge protocol, corresponding to approx. 3400 equivalent full cycles. A small (0.192 mΩ/1000 cycles) but continuous increase of internal resistance was observed. Using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), this could be identified to be caused by the NCA cathode, while the LTO anode showed only minor changes during cycling. The temperature-corrected capacity during 4C cycling exhibited a decrease of 1.28%/1000 cycles. The 1C discharge capacity faded by only 4.0% for CC discharge and 2.3% for CCCV discharge after 10,000 cycles. The cell thus exhibits very good internal-resistance stability and excellent capacity retention even under harsh (4C continuous) cycling, demonstrating the excellent stability of LTO as anode material.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years the demand for electrochemical storage systems for portable, mobile and stationary applications grew rapidly [1,2]. For stationary energy storage in industrial or private applications, high calendaric and cyclic lifetime is of particular importance [3].

The ageing of lithium-ion batteries is today considered to be strongly driven by the graphite negative electrode, which is subject to formation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) and can suffer from lithium plating when being charged at high current and/or low temperature [4,5]. An alternative negative electrode material is lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12, LTO) [6]. LTO is considered a “zero strain material”, that is, it shows negligible volume change during cycling and hence exhibits almost no mechanical aging [7–9]. In addition, due the comparatively high half-cell voltage (1.55 V vs. Li/Li+) as compared to graphite (0.05–0.3 V vs. Li/Li+), SEI formation (0.8 V vs. Li/Li+ [10]) and plating (0 V vs. Li/Li+) are effectively suppressed. Consequently, high cyclic lifetime of LTO electrodes has been demonstrated [11]. High half-cell voltage and low specific capacity (1.55 V vs. Li/Li+ and 180 Ah∙kg−1 [6], compared to 0.2 V vs. Li/Li+ and 370 Ah∙kg−1 [12] for graphite) reduce the cell-level specific energy considerably (57 Wh/kg for the cell considered in the present work), which however is not a key issue for stationary applications.

Various commercial lithium-ion cells have been subject of experimental investigations for assessing performance and lifetime under controlled laboratory environments. Graphite/LiFePO4 cells (C/LFP, high-power, 2.3 Ah, 26650) have been tested by Groot et al. [13], showing 20% capacity loss after 4200 cycles at 100% DOD, 1C, 20 °C. C/LFP cells (high power, 8 Ah, cylindrical) were also investigated by Lewerenz et al. [14], demonstrating a 25% capacity loss at 100% DOD, 1C, 40 °C after 4000 cycles. A graphite/nickel manganese cobalt oxide cell (C/NMC, high energy, 2.05 Ah, 18650) was tested by Ecker at al. [15], where cycling at 100% DOD, 1C and 35 °C showed a capacity fade of 20 % after only 500 cycles. In contrast, significantly higher lifetime was observed for cells with LTO anode. Hang et al. [16] observed a capacity fade of 12% after 5000 cycles at 100% DOD for high-power LTO/LCO (lithium cobalt oxide, 4 Ah) cells. Zaghib et al. showed negligible capacity fade for an LTO/LFP (18650 laboratory) cell after 20,000 full cycles [11]. Takami et al. [17] have investigated two prototype cells: An LTO/LMO (lithium manganese oxide) cell (3 Ah, high power, pouch) showed 4% capacity fade after 30,000 cycles with 10C rate at 100% DOD, 25 °C; and an LTO/NMC cell (20 Ah, high energy, prismatic) showed 14% capacity fade after 6000 cycles at 3C, 100% DOD, 25 °C. Yan et al. have investigated both C/LMO and LTO/NMC high-power cells using a dynamic hybrid electric vehicle cycling profile, where the LTO based cell showed significant higher cyclic lifetime than the graphite-based cell [18]. As all cells discussed here have different chemistries and format, a quantitative comparison is not possible. However, generally cells with an LTO negative electrode were observed to provide high cyclic lifetime.

In this present work we investigate the electrical, thermal and cycling behavior of commercial LTO/NCA cells designed for stationary energy storage. Continuous cycling at 4C (maximum rate) was performed for 10,000 cycles inbetween the nominal voltage limits (1.7–2.7 V). Internal resistance measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) carried out as checkup every 1000 cycles were used to support the identification of aging mechanisms. The experimental setup and used methods will be described in Section 2. In Section 3 electrical and thermal characteristics of the cell will be shown and discussed, followed by the results of the long-term cycling. In Section 4, capacity fade will be discussed and EIS spectra will be interpreted using an equivalent circuit.

Large-format full-cell measurements were carried out using commercial (Leclanché) lithium-ion pouch cells consisting of LTO as negative electrode, NCA as positive electrode, an aluminum oxide ceramic laminated type separator (produced by Leclanché), and copper and aluminum current collectors. The cells (163 × 174 × 12 mm3, 641 g) have a nominal capacity of 16 Ah, nominal voltage of 2.3 V, and nominal specific energy of 57 Wh/kg. The cells can be continuously charged and discharged at a maximum 4C-rate. Details of the electrode preparation and materials haven been published before [19] and are not repeated here for conciseness. The electrodes are balanced such that the NCA electrode is capacity-limiting towards low SOC, while the LTO electrode is capacity-limiting towards high SOC; LTO is operated within its plateau region for the complete SOC region below approx. 95% (cf. half-cell discharge curves in Ref. [19]). It is interesting to note that the LTO electrode can be seen as pseudo reference electrode, providing a constant (with respect to SOC, not with respect to temperature or C-rate) half-cell potential. The data shown here were collected from a total of four nominally identical individual cells.

The cells were cycled using a BaSyTec XCTS cell test system. This system has a resolution of 0.1 mV/0.2 mA. Temperature on the cell surface was measured at the center of the cell face using an NTC element, as provided with the BaSyTec system. The cells were placed in a CTS Type T −40/200 climate chamber using custom-made cell holders. Temperature inside the climate chamber was measured using a PT100 sensor which enables a temperature stability of ±0.3 K. EIS measurements were conducted using a Gamry Reference 3000 device.

Fresh cells as obtained from the manufacturer were characterized before long-term cycling tests were started. Charge/discharge curves using a constant current/constant voltage (CCCV) protocol for both charge (2.7 V/0.1C cutoff) and discharge (1.7 V/0.1C cutoff) were recorded at different symmetric C-rates (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 4C) and ambient temperatures (5, 20, 35, 50 °C). The rest time between charge and discharge was 30 min. We selected a symmetric CCCV protocol for the following reasons: (i) the full capacity range can be accessed, in particular with respect to the asymmetry of the discharge/charge behavior at low SOC (cf. below, Section 3.1); (ii) the data will be used in future for validating physicochemical models, where again a particular focus will need to be put on validating the behavior at low SOC [19]. Given the strong capacity-rate effect of the cell (cf. below, Section 3.1), CCCV discharge may also be relevant in applications; it is interesting to note that the cell is specified (data sheet) for symmetric 4C discharge and charge operation. Internal resistance R i was measured using a current step from I 1 = 1.6 A (0.1C) to
I 2
= 16
A
1 C
after discharging the cell (1C, 2 h rest) to the desired state of charge (SOC), defined based on the capacity measured at 1C/20 °C (varying with ageing time). From the measured voltage dynamics, R i was calculated according to
(1)
R i
= −
U
2
(
t
2
)
−
U
1
(
t
1
)
I
2
(
t
2
)
−
I
1
(
t
1
)
,

for a typical time step of
(2)
Δ
t =
t 2
−
t 1
= 3 s .

EIS measurements were performed in galvanostatic mode (I ac = 0.16 A, C/100) within a frequency range from 1 mHz to 10 kHz. From 10 kHz to 1 Hz ten data points per decade were registered, from 1 Hz to 1 mHz three data points per decade. We used Gamry Echem Analyst to fit an equivalent circuit model to the data.

For the cyclic ageing tests, the investigated cells were cycled for a total of 10,000 cycles between the nominal cutoff voltages of 1.7 V and 2.7 V with 4C, for both charge and discharge, without CV phase and without rest time in between cycles. This protocol was chosen to represent harsh cycling conditions. Note that the lack of the CV phase leads to considerably lower cycle depth (approx. 5.5 Ah cycled capacity corresponding to approx. 34% DOD). As will be further discussed below, it is mainly the low-SOC regime that is omitted with this cycling protocol.

After each 1000 cycles checkup measurements were carried out consisting of charge/discharge curves, internal resistance, and EIS at SOC = 50 %. The cells were cycled at an ambient temperature of 20 °C.

Fig. 1 shows results of symmetric charge/discharge measurements (voltage vs. capacity) for different conditions. Fig. 1a) compares different C-rates at 20 °C ambient temperature. The cell shows the typical increasing discharge and charge overpotentials with increasing current. As consequence, although the cells were cycled with a CCCV protocol, the CC discharge part of the capacity is considerably rate-dependent. More noteworthy, the cell shows a distinct asymmetry in the discharge/charge behavior towards low SOC (above approx. 13 Ah discharged capacity). The asymmetry is present even at low C-rates. We have previously shown that this behavior is due to the NCA positive electrode and can be caused by a two-step adsorption/intercalation mechanism [19]. At high C-rates an “overshoot” of the voltage at the beginning of the charging phase is observed. This effect may be related to the two-step mechanism or may have additional causes such as self-heating.

Fig. 1b) and c) compare the behavior at different temperatures. The cell exhibits a significant temperature dependence of overpotential, discharge capacity, and asymmetry. It is noteworthy that high-current (4C) charge is possible even at low ambient temperature of 5 °C. The observed “overshoot” at beginning of charge is pronounced at low temperature. Because the low-temperature discharge does not show such behavior, it is unlikely that self-heating is the (only) cause. The results at 0.1C show that the low-SOC region is particularly temperature-influenced. The observed loss of asymmetry at high temperature is associated with a strong increase of CC capacity from 16.3 Ah (5 °C) to 20.5 Ah (50 °C). Potential origins of this effect will be discussed below (Section 4.1).

Fig. 2 shows cell surface temperature as function of time corresponding to the conditions shown in Fig. 1. Panel a) shows the increasing temperature rise with increasing C-rate. Note that for 0.1 and 0.2C-rates temperature rise is below 0.5 °C, therefore the x-axis is cut at 7 h in order to provide a clearer presentation of the higher C-rate data. The temperature rise is asymmetric with respect to charge/discharge, where the charge phase (first peak) shows a lower peak temperature but longer temperature plateaus than the discharge phase (second peak). Fig. 2b shows the temperature rise at different ambient temperatures at 4C. The absolute temperature rise increases with decreasing ambient temperature due to increasing irreversible heat release (overpotentials). The shape of the curves is complex, owed to the consecutive CC/CV/rest protocol for both charge and discharge and the resulting combination of irreversible and reversible heating and cooling to the ambient. For example, the 5 °C data show a lower discharge (second) than charge (first) temperature peak because of the long and dominating CV phase (cf. Fig. 1b).

Fig. 2c shows the behavior at 0.1C, where the entropic heat is pronounced in comparison to the irreversible heat. These data show that the charge (first) phase is associated with entropic heating, while the discharge (second) phase with entropic cooling. This is consistent with half-cell entropy (dV/dT) measurements of NCA [20] and LTO [21]. For the LTO electrode, these data predict a small entropic cooling (dV/dT ≈ −5·10−5 V/K) during cell discharge. The NCA electrode, during cell discharge, initially shows large entropic cooling (dV/dT ≈ 2·10−4 V/K for SOC < approx. 60%) and then entropic heating (dV/dT ≈ − 3·10−4 V/K for SOC > approx. 70%). Towards end of discharge, this behavior is probably masked by irreversible heating due to high overpotentials associated with the asymmetry. Note that the observed temperature variation (approx. 0.2 °C) is below the rated temperature stability of the climate chamber (±0.3 °C), therefore a further quantitative interpretation is not possible. From this analysis of the low-C-rate behavior, we also conclude that the difference between charge and discharge temperature rise oberseved at higher C-rates (Fig. 2a) is not due to entropic heat consumption/release, but due to the asymmetric overpotentials (cf. Fig. 1) with considerably longer CV phase during discharge than during charge, causing stronger irreversible heating during discharge than during charge.

Taking into account the CC phase only, the influence of temperature on 4C discharge capacity is shown in Fig. 3 a). Here, the ambient temperature was varied between 5 and 50 °C in 5 °C steps. The capacity shows a sigmoidal behavior, approaching 0 Ah towards low temperature and approx. 17 Ah towards high temperature. In the intermediate temperature regime of around 30 °C the behavior can be assumed to be linear. Fig. 3b) shows results obtained during long-term cycling (cf. Section 3.2). During continuous 4C cycling, ambient temperature was slowly varied by changing the temperature control of the climate chamber from 20 °C to 19 °C, up to 21 °C, and back to 20 °C during a total time of 8 h. Each data point in Fig. 3b) corresponds to one cycle. The observed linear dependence between capacity and temperature can be quantified from a linear fit as 0.37 Ah ∙ K−1; this value will be used in Section 3.2 for temperature-correcting the measured cell capacity.

As additional characterization, the differential voltage (dV/dC) behavior was calculated [22–24]. In this representation a peak indicates a phase transition in the active material [25]. Fig. 4 shows the differential voltage for 0.1C discharge at different temperatures. These data demonstrate again the strong temperature dependence of the capacity. The two peaks observed at 20 °C at around 4 Ah and 11 Ah correspond to the characteristics of the NCA electrode, while the LTO electrode is balanced to operate within its plateau region except for the initial discharge region (peak at <1 Ah). At increasing temperature, a third peak grows at around 17.5 Ah. The potential origin of this peak will be discussed in Section 4.1.

A fresh cell was cycled for 10,000 cycles at 4C (charge and discharge) without CV phase and without rest between the nominal cutoff voltages. Note that 4C is the recommended maximum current rate for this cell. The cycling protocol thus represents harsh conditions.

Fig. 5 a shows the measured discharge capacity as function of cycle number. The capacity reaches values between 5 and 6 Ah, corresponding to the 4C constant-current capacity (Fig. 1a). Note that the lack of the CV phase leads to a cycle depth considerably lower than the nominal capacity (approx. 5.5 Ah cycled capacity versus 16 Ah nominal capacity corresponds to approx. 34% DOD), therefore the cell cycles for approx. 3400 equivalent full cycles. By measuring the full-charge capacity after cycling, the SOC cycling range was quantified to be 50…84%. Therefore, the chosen cycling protocol omits mainly the low-SOC range.

The capacity shows a “step”-like behavior every 1000 cycles, where the cycling was halted and detailed characterization was carried out. It shows furthermore a short-term “spike”-like structure; these spikes correspond to short interruptions of the climate control due to occasional interruptions of the in-house compressed air supply used for purging the climate chamber. Within the range of the “steps” and “spikes” no capacity loss over the complete 10,000 cycles is observed; in fact, a linear fit shows a capacity increase of 0.79%/1000 cycles.

Fig. 5b shows the cell surface temperature (averaged values during the discharge time). Apart from a similar “step” and “spike” structure, the temperature shows a small but significant continuous increase during cycling time. This is likely due to the continuous increase of internal resistance of the cell (cf. below), increasing the heat losses. We believe that the “steps” occurring every 1000 cycles are due to changes in the arrangement of (additional) cells within the climate chamber that was used in parallel for other tests, therefore inducing minor changes in the thermal boundary conditions for the investigated cell. Based on the available data, it is not possible to further quantify the influence of this thermal crosstalk between cells on cell performance and ageing behavior.

Given the strong temperature dependency of capacity discussed above (Fig. 3a), it is clear that the observed variation of temperature leads to variation of capacity which can easily mask capacity-loss due to cyclic ageing. In order to decouple temperature effects, we have calculated a temperature-corrected capacity
C i corr
according to
(3)
C i corr
=
C i
− m ·
(
T
i
−
T ¯
)
,

where, i is the cycle number, C
i the measured capacity, and
T ¯
= 30.6
° C
the average temperature over all 10,000 cycles. The capacity/temperature slope m = 0.37 Ah · K−1 was determined as described above (Section 3.1, Fig. 3b) at around 6500 cycles as marked with an asterisk in Fig. 5 and, lacking further experiments, was assumed to be constant over the complete test duration. The temperature-corrected capacity is shown in Fig. 5c. Both “steps” and “spikes” are significantly reduced as compared to the measured capacity. At the same time, an overall capacity loss becomes observable. A linear fit over the complete 10,000 cycles yields a capacity loss of 1.28%/1000 cycles. The last cycle shows a relative loss of capacity of 19.1 % compared to the first cycle. From the temperature-corrected capacity, it is also observed that the cell recovers after each 1000 cycles during the characterization measurements, in a way that the capacity at the beginning of the next set is higher than before. The origin of this effect is unclear.

After each 1000 cycles, a detailed checkup was carried out, consisting of charge/discharge cycles (symmetric CCCV protocol) at different (0.5, 1, 2, 4) C-rates, internal resistance measurements, and EIS. These experiments were carried out at 20 °C ambient temperature.

Discharge capacity for different C-rates is shown as function of the number of cycles in Fig. 6 . Here we evaluated both, the capacity for the full CCCV discharge (panel a) and the capacity of the CC part only (panel b). Both data show an excellent capacity retention. CCCV discharge shows a capacity loss of only 2–3% after 10,000 cycles, where higher discharge rates show a slightly higher capacity loss. CC discharge shows a capacity loss of 3.1, 4.0, 5.1 and 21.8% after 10,000 cycles for 0.5C, 1C, 2C and 4C, respectively. Higher C-rates therefore clearly show a pronounced capacity loss; the origin will be discussed below (Section 4.2). The general scatter of the data is believed to be due to limited measurement accuracy and small variation of thermal boundary conditions of the cells during checkup. The origin of the systematic scatter of the 2C and 4C data at 9000 cycles is unknown.

Fig. 7 a shows the internal resistance at 50% SOC as function of the number of cycles. A continuous increase is observed. The behavior can be approximated with a linear function with a slope of 0.192 mΩ/1000 cycles. EIS spectra are shown in Fig. 7b (Nyquist plot) and c (Bode plot). They show the typical features of lithium-ion cells with an inductive behavior at high frequency, (at least) two overlapping semi-circles at intermediate frequency, and a Warburg-like diffusion branch at low frequency. A continuous increase of the main intermediate-frequency semi-circle during cycling is observed, whereas the high-frequency behavior (including x-axis intercept) and the low-frequency branch change only slightly. A more detailed evaluation and interpretation of the EIS data will be given in Section 4.2.

The cell capacity exhibits a strong temperature dependence: Between 5 °C and 50 °C the cell shows a capacity gain of 4.2 Ah at 0.1C; based on the nominal capacity of 16 Ah, temperature-dependent capacity variation at 1C (CC) is −12.5% (5 °C) … +17.5% (50 °C) or 0.67%/°C. This is considerably higher than the LTO/NMC cell studied by Takami et al. [17], showing a capacity variation of −8% (0 °C) … + 7% (60 °C) or 0.25%/°C. We have shown above (Section 3.2) that the high temperature sensitivity can mask capacity loss during cycling when internal-resistance increase leads to an increase of cell temperatures.

The temperature dependence of the capacity has a few particular properties: (i) It is observed even at low (0.1) C-rate (Fig. 1c); (ii) the “sigmoidal-like” shape of C vs. T (Fig. 3a) is different than the usually-observed “square-root-like” shape with continuously increasing slope towards decreasing T [17,26]; and (iii) it is associated with the low-SOC regime (>90% SOC based on nominal capacity), where the differential voltage shows an additional peak growing at around 17.5 Ah at high temperature (Fig. 4). The mechanistic origin of this behavior is unclear. The observations are unlikely due to temperature-dependent overpotentials (e.g., kinetic or transport limitations), which should not have a large effect at low C-rates and should not lead to a major change in the differential voltage pattern. The low C-rate behavior (Fig. 1c) is typical for temperature-dependent overpotentials only at SOC <80%.

A possible alternative interpretation will be given in the following. The temperature sensitivity is associated with the low-SOC regime, which is also associated with the observed asymmetric charge/discharge behavior. Indeed, at high temperature the asymmetry is lost, which seems to provide the additional capacity. We have previously interpreted the asymmetry as two-stage adsorption-intercalation mechanism at the NCA cathode particle surface and have introduced an analytical model that includes surface-adsorbed species [19]. We therefore believe that the NCA cathode is responsible for the temperature sensitivity of the full cell.

Surface-adsorbed lithium can represent an additional chemical capacity. As surface thermodynamics, kinetics and structure can be strongly temperature dependent [27], the high-temperature capacity might be caused by an activation of surface-adsorbed lithium. We therefore estimate if the NCA particle surface can host the observed high-temperature capacity. Assuming geometric cell and electrode parameters (NCA electrode thickness: 50 μm, internal cell separator area: 1.2 m2), the capacity increase of 4.2 Ah corresponds to
n V
= 2.6 ·
10
3
mol
lithium
m
3
NCA
volume
. Assuming a specific surface site density (i.e., density of a monolayer of adsorbed lithium atoms) of
Γ = 5 ·
10
6
mol
m 2
(approximate value for metal oxide surfaces [28]), this corresponds to a volume-specific surface area of
A V
=
n V
Γ
= 5.2 ·
10
8
m 2
m 3
. Further assuming an NCA volume fraction of ε = 0.5 and spherical particles for which A
V = 3ε/r [29], the corresponding NCA particle radius is r = 2.9 nm. In conclusion, NCA particles with a radius of 2.9 nm can host surface lithium corresponding to a cell-level capacity of 4.2 Ah. However, this small size is not realistic for this electrode. This order-of-magnitude estimation thus shows that a monolayer of surface-adsorbed lithium cannot account for the observed capacity increase.

This changes if we assume the presence of a lithium-containing surface film instead of a surface monolayer. Assuming that the film consists of 100 atomic layers, the site density Γ increases by a factor of 100, A
V decreases by the same factor, and consequently the required particle radius would increase to r = 290 nm. This is a realistic value for primary particles in an agglomerate structure. We therefore postulate the presence of a thin surface layer on top of the NCA particles, containing lithium (e.g., in the form of an oxide) that can be activated upon increased temperature, causing an additional feature in the differential voltage, and causing the strong temperature sensitivity. The verification of this postulate would require further experimental investigations (e.g., high-resolution transmission electron microscopy of electrode particles discharged into the high-temperature high-capacity region, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy at elevated temperature and low SOC) or modeling studies (e.g., borrowed from SEI growth and transport models at graphite anodes [30]), which are, however, out of scope of the present study. The presence of surface layers on NCA particles will be discussed below as potential ageing mechanism.

The discharge data (Fig. 6) show a significant dependence of the capacity loss on the discharge rate. At high constant-current C-rates, the capacity loss is considerably faster than for low C-rates. If a CV phase is added, the capacity loss becomes comparable for all C-rates. From these results we conclude that internal-resistance increase is the dominating ageing effect, while loss of cycleable capacity (due to loss of lithium inventory and/or due to de-balancing of the electrodes) is of less importance. This is consistent with the increase of internal resistance during cycling (Fig. 7a). In other words, the cell mainly loses rate capability, and only to a lesser extent capacity.

In order to further investigate the origin of the internal-resistance increase, an equivalent circuit model was used to evaluate the measured EI spectra. The model is shown in the inset of Fig. 8 d. Here, R s is the ohmic resistance of ion transport in the electrolyte and electron transport in the current collection system of the cell; the two RC elements represent charge-transfer resistance and electrochemical double layer at anode and cathode; Z w is a Warburg impedance [31] representing a diffusion process; and L is the inductance caused by the measurement setup. Fig. 8a shows calculated spectra after fitting to the experiments. The equivalent circuit is able to provide a good agreement with the measurement data, except for the low-frequency part where the experiments do not show an ideal Warburg behavior.

Fig. 9 shows measured electrochemical impedance of a fresh cell at 20 °C at different SOC. These data show a strong increase of the low-frequency feature at low SOC, which we showed before to be associated with the NCA cathode [19]. Based on this observation, we assign the small RC semicircle at high frequency to the LTO anode and the larger low-frequency feature to the NCA cathode.

The results of the equivalent circuit analysis are plotted as function of the number of cycles in Fig. 8b–d. There are significant differences in the ageing behavior of the different cell components. Fig. 8b shows the resistances. The serial resistance R s remains constant over the complete cycling duration. Anode resistance R an increases during the first 2000 cycles and remains constant afterwards. A significant increase is observed only for the cathode resistance R ca, namely with an average slope of 0.116 mΩ/1000 cycles. Fig. 8c shows the double-layer capacitances, where again C an remains constant while C ca increases. The Warburg coefficient shown in Fig. 8d shows only a minor increase over cycling time. From the combined results we conclude that the NCA cathode is dominating the cell-level internal-resistance increase, which also comes along with an increase of the double-layer capacitance, while the LTO anode shows only a small contribution to ageing within the first 2000 cycles, which is not associated with changes in double-layer capacitance.

The results thus confirm the excellent cyclic lifetime of LTO as anode material, which is due to (cf. introduction) the significant reduction of both, SEI formation due to the high half-cell potential, and mechanical ageing due to low volume-change, as compared to a standard graphite electrode. The results also show a reduced, but still high cyclic lifetime of NCA as cathode material. Widely reported has been a formation of a NiO-like layer on the surface of the NCA particles [20,32,33]. In aged cells with NCA cathodes a divalent NiO layer was found [34]. This reaction is reported to be irreversible [35]. A surface layer formation will lead to an increase of impedance [4], which can be clearly seen in our results.

Growth of a NiO-like layer should also provoke capacity fade due to the loss of active lattice sites [36]. Another proposed aging mechanism is the occurrence of structural changes caused by microstrains during cycling [37]. These irreversible changes increase from cycle to cycle and lead to a structural disordering. This mechanism will lead to a capacity fade [4]. According to the present results, these two effects are only minor in the investigated cells.

Lithium-ion batteries for stationary energy storage need to provide a high cycle and calendar life in order to be economically viable. In this study commercial 16 Ah LTO/NCA pouch cells have been experimentally characterized thermally and electrically at different ambient temperatures and C-rates. The cell shows an asymmetric charge/discharge behavior which leads to a strong capacity-rate effect and which we have interpreted before as two-step adsorption/intercalation mechanism at the NCA cathode [19]. The cell also exhibits an unusually strong temperature-dependent capacity (0.37 Ah ∙ K−1 at around 30 °C), which expresses as additional high-temperature feature in the differential voltage plot. We suggest, without verification, the presence of a lithium-containing film on the surface of the NCA cathode particles as potential cause for the temperature sensitivity.

In order to characterize cyclic ageing behavior, the cell was undergone 10,000 continuous cycles inbetween the nominal voltage limits (1.7–2.7 V) at a symmetric 4C charge and discharge rate without CV phase and without rest, corresponding to approx. 3400 equivalent full cycles. After correcting for the temperature dependence, the capacity fade during cycling was quantified to 1.28%/1000 cycles. After each 1000 cycles checkup measurements were carried out, consisting of charge/discharge cycles at different C-rates, internal resistance measurements, and EIS. The 1C discharge capacity faded by only 4.0% for CC discharge and 2.3% for CCCV discharge after 10,000 cycles. The main ageing characteristics is a moderate increase of internal resistance from 3.4 mΩ (fresh cell) to 5.5 mΩ (10,000 cycles). The cell thus exhibits excellent capacity retention and very good internal-resistance stability even under harsh (4C continuous) cycling.

In order to investigate the origin of ageing in more detail, the EI spectra were evaluated using an equivalent circuit. As a result, the NCA cathode could be identified as dominating ageing component, exhibiting an increase of both charge-transfer resistance and double-layer capacitance. These observations are consistent with the formation and/or growth of a nickel oxide layer on top of the NCA active material particle which has been suggested before as ageing mechanism of NCA. The LTO anode shows a small increase of charge-transfer resistance only within the first 2000 cycles and negligible change of double-layer capacitance. These results confirm the high cyclic stability of LTO as negative electrode material [11,16,17].

Although the present article includes qualitative mechanistic interpretations, the results and discussion are mainly empirical. However, the data provide a good basis for physically-based modeling approaches [29,38], which can be carried out in follow-up work in order to provide an improved understanding of the cell behavior.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the German Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) in the framework of the TempOLadung project (Grant no. 03FH013PX3) is gratefully acknowledged. The authors thank Sebastian Ewinger and Christian Kupper (Offenburg University of Applied Sciences) for their support.
